author_background: Former marine biologist and university professor specializing in coral reef ecosystems. Married to Lena with two daughters - Maya (8) and Chloe (6). Was writing a book about the Great Barrier Reef's recovery patterns. Dreamed of taking his family sailing around the world to study marine life.
location: Abandoned coastal research station, Pacific Northwest. Rusting equipment everywhere, salt-crusted windows overlooking churning gray waters, the constant smell of decay and seaweed. Generator hums irregularly in the background.
entry:
The rain hasn't stopped for three days. It drums against the corrugated metal roof in a rhythm that's become the soundtrack to my madness. I keep finding Maya's drawings tucked in unexpected places - behind equipment panels, between pages of waterlogged research journals. Her little mermaids with smiling faces seem to mock me from another lifetime.

Yesterday I almost shot a scavenger. Just a kid, probably no older than Maya would be now. He was trying to break into our food storage, skin and bones wrapped in rags. My finger was on the trigger when I saw the way his hands shook - exactly like Chloe's did when she had a fever. I let him take two cans of beans and watched him scramble back into the mist.

Lena would have called me soft. She was always the practical one. 'Survival isn't pretty, Aris,' she'd say while teaching the girls how to properly clean a fish. God, I can still see the concentration on Chloe's face as her small hands worked the knife, tongue sticking out just like her mother's.

The infection took them so fast. One day Maya was complaining about math homework, the next she was coughing up black phlegm. I failed them as a husband, as a father, as a scientist. All my knowledge of marine ecosystems meant nothing against a microscopic enemy.

Sometimes I stand at the water's edge and talk to the empty ocean. The whales have gone silent. No more dolphin pods playing in the bay. The world didn't end with bombs or zombies - it's just... emptying. Becoming a ghost of what it was.

That boy today - I should have brought him in. Given him shelter. But fear is a colder companion than loneliness, and I've grown accustomed to its company.
condition: Physically: Malnourished but functional, right knee aches in the dampness, hands permanently stained with fish scales and engine grease. Mentally: Haunted by grief, sleeps in brief fits, talks to himself and the ghosts of his family. Moments of clarity alternate with deep depression.
discoveries: The infected avoid salt water - something about the salinity disrupts the pathogen's transmission. Coastal areas might be safer than inland. Also found that certain seaweed varieties are still edible and packed with nutrients the body desperately needs.
warnings: Don't trust clear water streams - the pathogen survives in fresh water for weeks. Always boil or add seawater. The infected are drawn to smoke - cook during daylight only. And for God's sake, don't go near hospitals or clinics - they're death traps filled with the worst concentrations of the sick.
last_meal: Steamed kelp and a small rockfish caught this morning, eaten around 10 hours ago. Seasoned with dried sea salt from our evaporator.
companions: Alone. Lost wife Lena and daughters Maya and Chloe to the outbreak in the first month. Occasionally trades with a fishing family that passes by every few weeks, but they keep their distance.
